content:
It is assumed that all configuration commands start with the keyword “config” as prefix. Any other scripts/utilities/commands  that need user configuration control are wrapped as sub-commands under the “config” command. The direct scripts/utilities/commands (examples given below) that are not wrapped under the "config" command are not in the scope of this document. 1. acl_loader – This script is already wrapped inside “config acl” command; i.e. any ACL configuration that user is allowed to do is already part of “config acl” command; users are not expected to use the acl_loader script directly and hence this document need not explain the “acl_loader” script. 2. crm – this command is not explained in this document. 3. sonic-clear, sfputil, etc., This document does not explain these scripts also.
Command: config --help

This command displays the full list of show commands available in the software; the output of each of those show commands can be used to analyze, debug or troubleshoot the network node.
Command: show (-?|-h|--help)

This command displays software component versions of the currently running SONiC image. This includes the SONiC image version as well as Docker image versions. This command displays relevant information as the SONiC and Linux kernel version being utilized, as well as the ID of the commit used to build the SONiC image. The second section of the output displays the various docker images and their associated IDs.
Command: show version

This command displays the current date and time configured on the system
Command: show clock

This command will set the date-time of the systetm, given strings with date-time format <YYYY-MM-DD> <HH:MM:SS>
Command: config clock date <YYYY-MM-DD> <HH:MM:SS>

This command will set the timezone of the systetm, given a string of a valid timezone.
Command: config clock timezone <timezone>

This command Will display list of all valid timezones to be configured.
Command: show clock timezones

This command displays the current OS image, the image to be loaded on next reboot, and lists all the available images installed on the device
Command: show boot

This command displays the platform environmentals, such as voltages, temperatures and fan speeds
Command: show environment

This command displays the cause of the previous reboot
Command: show reboot-cause

This command displays the cause of the previous reboot for the Switch and the DPUs for which the midplane interfaces are up.
Command: show reboot-cause all

This command displays the history of the previous reboots up to 10 entry
Command: show reboot-cause history

This command displays the history of the previous reboots up to 10 entry of the Switch and the DPUs for which the midplane interfaces are up.
Command: show reboot-cause history all

This command displays the history of the previous reboots up to 10 entry of DPU1. If DPU1 is powered down then there won't be any data in the DB and the "show reboot-cause history DPU1" output will be blank.
Command: show reboot-cause history DPU1

This command displays the current system uptime
Command: show uptime

This command displays all the currently stored log messages. All the latest processes and corresponding transactions are stored in the "syslog" file. This file is saved in the path `/var/log` and can be viewed by giving the command ` sudo cat syslog` as this requires root login.
Command: show logging [(<process_name> [-l|--lines <number_of_lines>]) | (-f|--follow)]

This command displays a list of users currently logged in to the device
Command: show users

This command displays a summary of the device's hardware platform
Command: show platform summary

This command displays information stored on the system EEPROM. Note that the output of this command is not the same for all vendor's platforms. Couple of example outputs are given below.
Command: show platform syseeprom

This command displays health parameters of the device's SSD
Command: show platform ssdhealth [--vendor]

This command displays the status of the device's power supply units
Command: show platform psustatus

This command displays the status of the device's fans
Command: show platform fan

This command displays the status of the device's thermal sensors
Command: show platform temperature

This command displays information for all the interfaces for the transceiver requested or a specific interface if the optional "interface_name" is specified.
Command: show interfaces transceiver (eeprom [-d|--dom] | info | lpmode | presence | error-status [-hw|--fetch-from-hardware] | pm | status) [<interface_name>]

This command displays the AAA settings currently present in the network node
Command: show aaa

This command is used to either enable or disable the failthrough option. This command is useful when user has configured more than one tacacs+ server and when user has enabled tacacs+ authentication. When authentication request to the first server fails, this configuration allows to continue the request to the next server. When this configuration is enabled, authentication process continues through all servers configured. When this is disabled and if the authentication request fails on first server, authentication process will stop and the login will be disallowed.
Command: config aaa authentication failthrough (enable | disable | default)

The command is not used at the moment. When the tacacs+ authentication fails, it falls back to local authentication by default.
Command: config aaa authentication fallback (enable | disable | default)

This command is used to either configure whether AAA should use local database or remote tacacs+ database for user authentication. By default, AAA uses local database for authentication. New users can be added/deleted using the linux commands (Note that the configuration done using linux commands are not preserved during reboot). Admin can enable remote tacacs+ server based authentication by selecting the AUTH_PROTOCOL as tacacs+ in this command. Admins need to configure the tacacs+ server accordingly and ensure that the connectivity to tacacas+ server is available via the management interface. Once if the admins choose the remote authentication based on tacacs+ server, all user logins will be authenticated by the tacacs+ server. If the authentication fails, AAA will check the "failthrough" configuration and authenticates the user based on local database if failthrough is enabled.
Command: config aaa authentication (tacacs+ | local | default)

This command displays the global configuration fields and the list of all tacacs servers and their correponding configurations.
Command: show tacacs

This command is used to add a TACACS+ server to the tacacs server list. Note that more than one tacacs+ (maximum of seven) can be added in the device. When user tries to login, tacacs client shall contact the servers one by one. When any server times out, device will try the next server one by one based on the priority value configured for that server. When this command is executed, the configured tacacs+ server addresses are updated in /etc/pam.d/common-auth-sonic configuration file which is being used by tacacs service.
Command: config tacacs add <ip_address> [-t|--timeout <seconds>] [-k|--key <secret>] [-a|--type <type>] [-o|--port <port>] [-p|--pri <priority>] [-m|--use-mgmt-vrf]

This command is used to delete the tacacs+ servers configured.
Command: config tacacs delete <ip_address>

This command is used to modify the global value for the TACACS+ authtype. When user has not configured server specific authtype, this global value shall be used for that server.
Command: config tacacs authtype (chap | pap | mschap | login)

This command is used to reset the global value for authtype or passkey or timeout to default value. Default for authtype is "pap", default for passkey is EMPTY_STRING and default for timeout is 5 seconds.
Command: config tacacs default (authtype | passkey | timeout)

This command is used to modify the global value for the TACACS+ passkey. When user has not configured server specific passkey, this global value shall be used for that server.
Command: config tacacs passkey <pass_key>

This command is used to modify the global value for the TACACS+ timeout. When user has not configured server specific timeout, this global value shall be used for that server.
Command: config tacacs [default] timeout [<timeout_value_in_seconds>]

This command displays either all the ACL tables that are configured or only the specified "TABLE_NAME". Output from the command displays the table name, type of the table, the list of interface(s) to which the table is bound and the description about the table.
Command: show acl table [<table_name>]

This command displays all the ACL rules present in all the ACL tables or only the rules present in specified table "TABLE_NAME" or only the rule matching the RULE_ID option. Output from the command gives the following information about the rules 1) Table name - ACL table name to which the rule belongs to. 2) Rule name - ACL rule name 3) Priority - Priority for this rule. 4) Action - Action to be performed if the packet matches with this ACL rule.
Command: show acl rule [<table_name>] [<rule_id>]

This command is to update the rules in all the tables or in one specific table in full. If a table_name is provided, the operation will be restricted in the specified table. All existing rules in the specified table or all tables will be removed. New rules loaded from file will be installed. If the table_name is specified, only rules within that table will be removed and new rules in that table will be installed. If the table_name is not specified, all rules from all tables will be removed and only the rules present in the input file will be added.
Command: config acl update full [--table_name <table_name>] [--session_name <session_name>] [--mirror_stage (ingress | egress)] [--max_priority <priority_value>] <acl_json_file_name>

This command is used to perform incremental update of ACL rule table. This command gets existing rules from Config DB and compares with rules specified in input file and performs corresponding modifications.
Command: config acl update incremental [--session_name <session_name>] [--mirror_stage (ingress | egress)] [--max_priority <priority_value>] <acl_json_file_name>

This command is used to create new ACL tables.
Command: config acl add table [OPTIONS] <table_name> <table_type> [-d <description>] [-p <ports>] [-s (ingress | egress)]

This command is used to display: ACL rules, tables and their priority, ACL packets counters, and bytes counters
Command: aclshow [-h] [-a] [-c] [-r RULES] [-t TABLES] [-v] [-vv]

This command is for a customer to configure the categories that he/she wants to suppress for a certain severity.
Command: config config asic-sdk-health-event suppress <severity> [--category-list <category-list>|<none>|<all>] [--max-events <max-events>]

This command displays the received ASIC/SDK health events.
Command: show asic-sdk-health-event received [-n <asicname>]

This command displays the suppressed category list and maximum number of events of ASIC/SDK health events.
Command: show asic-sdk-health-event suppressed-category-list [-n <asicname>]

This command clears all the received ASIC/SDK health events.
Command: sonic-clear asic-sdk-health-event [-n <asicname>]

This command displays the ARP entries in the device with following options. 1) Display the entire table. 2) Display the ARP entries learnt on a specific interface. 3) Display the ARP of a specific ip-address.
Command: show arp [-if <interface_name>] [<ip_address>]

This command displays either all the IPv6 neighbor mac addresses, or for a particular IPv6 neighbor, or for all IPv6 neighbors reachable via a specific interface.
Command: show ndp [-if|--iface <interface_name>] <ipv6_address>

This command displays the state and key parameters of all BFD sessions.
Command: show bfd summary [-n <namespace>]

This command displays the state and key parameters of all BFD sessions that match an IP address.
Command: show bfd peer <peer-ip> [-n <namespace>]

This command displays all the details of IPv4 Border Gateway Protocol (BGP) prefixes.
Command: show ip bgp network [[<ipv4-address>|<ipv4-prefix>] [(bestpath | multipath | longer-prefixes | json)]]

This command displays the routing policy that takes precedence over the other route processes that are configured.
Command: show route-map

This command is used to show the status of suppress pending FIB feature. When enabled, BGP will not advertise routes which aren't yet offloaded.
Command: show suppress-fib-pending

This command displays BGP device global configuration.
Command: show bgp device-global

This command is used to shutdown all the BGP IPv4 & IPv6 sessions. When the session is shutdown using this command, BGP state in "show ip bgp summary" is displayed as "Idle (Admin)"
Command: config bgp shutdown all

This command is to shut down a BGP session with a neighbor by that neighbor's IP address or hostname
Command: sudo config bgp shutdown neighbor (<ip_address> | <hostname>)

This command is used to start up all the IPv4 & IPv6 BGP neighbors
Command: config bgp startup all

This command is used to start up the particular IPv4 or IPv6 BGP neighbor using either the IP address or hostname.
Command: config bgp startup neighbor (<ip-address> | <hostname>)

This command is used to remove particular IPv4 or IPv6 BGP neighbor configuration using either the IP address or hostname.
Command: config bgp remove neighbor <neighbor_ip_or_hostname>

This command is used to enable or disable announcements of routes not yet installed in the HW. Once enabled, BGP will not advertise routes which aren't yet offloaded.
Command: config suppress-fib-pending <enabled|disabled>

This command is used to manage BGP device global configuration.
Command: config bgp device-global tsa <enabled|disabled>
  config bgp device-global w-ecmp <enabled|disabled>

This command displays serial port or a virtual network connection status.
Command: show line (-b|--breif)

This command is used to enable SONiC console switch feature.
Command: config console enable

This command is used to disable SONiC console switch feature.
Command: config console disable

This command is used to add a console port setting.
Command: config console add <port_name> [--baud|-b <baud_rate>] [--flowcontrol|-f] [--devicename|-d <remote_device>]

This command is used to remove a console port setting.
Command: config console del <port_name>

This command is used to update the remote device name for a console port.
Command: config console remote_device <port_name> <remote_device>

This command is used to update the baud rate for a console port.
Command: config console baud <port_name> <baud_rate>

This command is used to enable or disable flow control feature for a console port.
Command: config console flow_control {enable|disable} <port_name>

This command allows user to connect to a remote device via console line with an interactive cli.
Command: connect line <target> (-d|--devicename)

This command allows user to connect to a remote device via console line with an interactive cli.
Command: connect device <devicename>

This command allows user to connect to a remote device via console line with an interactive cli.
Command: sonc-clear line <target> (-d|--devicename)

This command allows user to connect to a DPU serial console via TTY device with interactive CLI program: picocom. The configuration is from platform.json. The utility works only on smart switch that provides DPU UART connections through /dev/ttyS* devices.
Command: dpu-tty.py (-n|--name) <DPU_NAME> [(-b|-baud) <BAUD_RATE>] [(-t|-tty) <TTY>]

This command is used for downloading firmware tp upgrade the transciever module.
Command: sfputil firmware download PORT_NAME FILE_PATH

This command is used to start and run a downloaded image. This command transfers control from the currently running firmware to a new firmware.
Command: sfputil firmware run PORT_NAME

This command to commit the running image so that the module will boot from it on future boots.
Command: sfputil firmware commit PORT_NAME

This command is used to show ipv4 dhcp_relay helper.
Command: show dhcp_relay ipv4 helper

This command is used to show ipv6 dhcp_relay destination.
Command: show dhcp_relay ipv6 destination

This command is used to show ipv6 dhcp_relay counters.
Command: show dhcp_relay ipv6 counters

This command is used to show ipv4 dhcp_relay counters
Command: show dhcp_relay ipv4 counters [--dir (TX|RX)] [--type <type>] [<vlan_interface>]
Options:
  --dir [TX|RX]
  --type [Unknown|Discover|Offer|Request|Decline|Ack|Nak|Release|Inform|Bootp]

This command is used to clear ipv6 dhcp_relay counters.
Command: sonic-clear dhcp_relay ipv6 counters [-i <interface>]

This command is used to clear ipv4 dhcp_relay counters.
Command: sonic-clear dhcp_relay ipv4s counter [-i <interface>] [--dir (TX|RX)] [--type <type>]
  Options:
    -i, --interface TEXT
    --dir [TX|RX]
    --type [Unknown|Discover|Offer|Request|Decline|Ack|Nak|Release|Inform|Bootp]

This command is used to add a DHCP Relay Destination IP address or multiple IP addresses to a VLAN.  Note that more than one DHCP Relay Destination IP address can be added on a VLAN interface.
Command: config vlan dhcp_relay add <vlan_id> <dhcp_relay_destination_ips>

This command is used to delete a configured DHCP Relay Destination IP address or multiple IP addresses from a VLAN interface.
Command: config vlan dhcp_relay del <vlan-id> <dhcp_relay_destination_ips>

This command is used to add or delete IPv4 DHCP Relay helper addresses to a VLAN. Note that more than one DHCP Relay helper addresses can be operated on a VLAN interface.
Command: config dhcp_relay ipv4 helper (add | del) <vlan_id> <dhcp_helper_ips>

This command is used to add or del IPv6 DHCP Relay destination addresses to a VLAN. Note that more than one DHCP Relay Destination addresses can be operated on a VLAN interface.
Command: config dhcp_relay ipv6 destination (add | del) <vlan_id> <dhcp_destination_ips>

This command is used to show dhcp_server config. - Usage ``` show dhcp_server ipv4 info [--with_customize_option] [<dhcp_interface>] ```
Command: show dropcounters capabilities

This command is used to show the current running configuration of the drop counters on this device.
Command: show dropcounters configuration [-g <group name>]

This command is used to show the current statistics for the configured drop counters. Standard drop counters are displayed as well for convenience.
Command: show dropcounters counts [-g <group name>] [-t <counter type>]

This command is used to initialize a new drop counter. The user must specify a name, type, and initial list of drop reasons.
Command: config dropcounters install <counter name> <counter type> <reasons list> [-d <description>] [-g <group>] [-a <alias>]

This command is used to add drop reasons to an already initialized counter.
Command: config dropcounters add_reasons <counter name> <reasons list>

This command is used to remove drop reasons from an already initialized counter.
Command: config dropcounters remove_reasons <counter name> <reasons list>

This command is used to delete a drop counter.
Command: config dropcounters delete <counter name>

This comnmand is used to clear drop counters. This is done on a per-user basis.
Command: sonic-clear dropcounters

This command is used to configure the shared headroom pool. The shared headroom pool can be enabled in the following ways:
Command: config buffer shared-headroom-pool over-subscribe-ratio <over-subscribe-ratio>
  config buffer shared-headroom-pool size <size>

This command is used to configure a lossless buffer profile.
Command: config buffer profile add <profile_name> --xon <xon_threshold> --xoff <xoff_threshold> [-size <size>] [-dynamic_th <dynamic_th_value>] [-pool <ingress_lossless_pool_name>]
  config buffer profile set <profile_name> --xon <xon_threshold> --xoff <xoff_threshold> [-size <size>] [-dynamic_th <dynamic_th_value>] [-pool <ingress_lossless_pool_name>]
  config buffer profile remove <profile_name>

This command is used to configure the length of the cable connected to a port. The cable_length is in unit of meters and must be suffixed with "m".
Command: config interface cable_length <interface_name> <cable_length>

This command is used to configure the priority groups on which lossless traffic runs.
Command: config interface buffer priority-group lossless add <interface_name> <pg_map> [profile]
  config interface buffer priority-group lossless set <interface_name> <pg_map> [profile]
  config interface buffer priority-group lossless remove <interface_name> [<pg_map>]

This command is used to configure the buffer profiles for queues.
Command: config interface buffer queue add <interface_name> <queue_map> <profile>
  config interface buffer queue set <interface_name> <queue_map> <profile>
  config interface buffer queue remove <interface_name> <queue_map>

This command is used to display the status of buffer pools and profiles currently deployed to the ASIC.
Command: show buffer information

This command is used to display the status of buffer pools and profiles currently configured.
Command: show buffer configuration

This command displays all the WRED profiles that are configured in the device.
Command: show ecn

This command configures the possible fields in a particular WRED profile that is specified using "-profile <profilename>" argument. The list of the WRED profile fields that are configurable is listed in the below "Usage".
Command: config ecn -profile <profile_name> [-rmax <red_threshold_max>] [-rmin <red_threshold_min>] [-ymax <yellow_threshold_max>] [-ymin <yellow_threshold_min>] [-gmax <green_threshold_max>] [-gmin <green_threshold_min>] [-v|--verbose]

The below two commands can be used to manually isolate and unisolate a fabric link.
Command: config fabric port isolate [OPTIONS] <portid>
  config fabric port unisolate [OPTIONS] <portid>

This command sets a fabric link monitoring error threshold
Command: config fabric port monitor error threshold [OPTIONS] <crcCells> <rxCells>

This command sets the number of consecutive polls in which the threshold needs to be detected to isolate a link
Command: config fabric port monitor poll threshold isolation [OPTIONS] <pollCount>

This command sets the number of consecutive polls in which no error is detected to unisolate a link
Command: config fabric port monitor poll threshold recovery [OPTIONS] <pollCount>

This command sets the monitor state in CONFIG_DB to enable/disable the fabric monitor feature.
Command: config fabric port monitor state [OPTIONS] <state>

Shows the config of given feature or all if no feature is given. The "fallback" is shown only if configured. The fallback defaults to "true" when not configured.
Command: show feature config [<feature name>]

Shows the status of given feature or all if no feature is given. The "fallback" defaults to "true" when not configured. The subset of features are configurable for remote management and only those report additional data.
Command: show feature status [<feature name>]

Configures the owner for a feature as "local" or  "kube". The "local" implies starting the feature container from local image. The "kube" implies that kubernetes server is made eligible to deploy the feature. The deployment of a feature by kubernetes is conditional based on many factors like, whether the kube server is configured or not, connected-to-kube-server or not and if that master has manifest for this feature for this switch or not and more. At some point in future, the deployment *could* happen and till that point the feature can run from local image, called "fallback". The fallback is allowed by default and it could be toggled to "not allowed". When fallback is not allowed, the feature would run only upon deployment by kubernetes master.
Command: config feature owner [<feature name>] [local/kube]

Features configured for "kube" deployment could be allowed to fallback to using local image, until the point of successful kube deployment. The fallback is allowed by default.
Command: config feature fallback [<feature name>] [on/off]

This command will display the status of auto-restart for feature container.
Command: show feature autorestart [<feature_name>]
  admin@sonic:~$ show feature autorestart
  Feature     AutoRestart
  ----------  --------------
  bgp         enabled
  database    always_enabled
  dhcp_relay  enabled
  lldp        enabled
  pmon        enabled
  radv        enabled
  snmp        enabled
  swss        enabled
  syncd       enabled
  teamd       enabled
  telemetry   enabled

This command will configure the state for a specific feature.
Command: config feature state <feature_name> (enabled | disabled)
  admin@sonic:~$ sudo config feature state bgp disabled

This command will configure the status of auto-restart for a specific feature container.
Command: config feature autorestart <feature_name> (enabled | disabled)
  admin@sonic:~$ sudo config feature autorestart bgp disabled

This command is used to show the current statistics for the registered host interface traps.
Command: show flowcnt-trap stats

This command is used to show the current statistics for route flow patterns.
Command: show flowcnt-route stats
  show flowcnt-route stats pattern <route_pattern> [--vrf <vrf>]
  show flowcnt-route stats route <route_prefix> [--vrf <vrf>]

This command is used to clear the current statistics for the registered host interface traps. This is done on a per-user basis.
Command: sonic-clear flowcnt-trap

This command is used to clear the current statistics for the route flow counter. This is done on a per-user basis.
Command: sonic-clear flowcnt-route
  sonic-clear flowcnt-route pattern <route_pattern> [--vrf <vrf>]
  sonic-clear flowcnt-route route <prefix> [--vrf <vrf>]

This command is used to add or update the route pattern which is used by route flow counter to match route entries.
Command: config flowcnt-route pattern add <prefix> [--vrf <vrf>] [--max <max_match_count>]

This command is used to remove the route pattern which is used by route flow counter to match route entries.
Command: config flowcnt-route pattern remove <prefix> [--vrf <vrf>]

This command displays information about the gearbox phy interface lanes, speeds and status. Data is displayed for both MAC side and line side of the gearbox phy
Command: show gearbox interfaces status

This command displays basic information about the gearbox phys configured on the switch.
Command: show gearbox phys status

This command is used to change device hostname without traffic being impacted.
Command: config hostname <new_hostname>

This command displays switch hash global configuration.
Command: show switch-hash global

This command displays switch hash capabilities.
Command: show switch-hash capabilities

This command is used to manage switch hash global configuration.
Command: config switch-hash global ecmp-hash <hash_field_list>
  config switch-hash global lag-hash <hash_field_list>

This command is used to manage switch hash algorithm global configuration.
Command: config switch-hash global ecmp-hash-algorithm <hash_algorithm>
  config switch-hash global lag-hash-algorithm <hash_algorithm>

This show command displays the port auto negotiation status for all interfaces i.e. interface name, auto negotiation mode, speed, advertised speeds, interface type, advertised interface types, operational status, admin status. For a single interface, provide the interface name with the sub-command.
Command: show interfaces autoneg status
  show interfaces autoneg status <interface_name>

This show command displays the port capability for all interfaces i.e. index, lanes, default_brkout_mode, breakout_modes(i.e. available breakout modes) and brkout_mode (i.e. current breakout mode). To display current breakout mode, "current-mode" subcommand can be used.For a single interface, provide the interface name with the sub-command.
Command: show interfaces breakout
  show interfaces breakout current-mode
  show interfaces breakout current-mode <interface_name>

This show command displays packet counters for all interfaces(except the "show interface detailed" command) since the last time the counters were cleared. To display l3 counters "rif" subcommand can be used. There is no facility to display counters for one specific l2 interface. For l3 interfaces a single interface output mode is present.  Optional argument "-a" provides two additional columns - RX-PPS and TX_PPS. Optional argument "-p" specify a period (in seconds) with which to gather counters over. To display the detailed per-interface counters "detailed <interface-name>" subcommand can be used.
Command: show interfaces counters [-a|--printall] [-p|--period <period>]
  show interfaces counters errors
  show interfaces counters rates
  show interfaces counters rif [-p|--period <period>] [-i <interface_name>]
  show interfaces counters fec-histogram [-i <interface_name>]
  show interfaces counters fec-stats
  show interfaces counters detailed <interface_name>
  show interfaces counters trim [interface_name] [-p|--period <sec>] [-j|--json]

This command displays the key fields of the interfaces such as Operational Status, Administrative Status, Alias and Description.
Command: show interfaces description [<interface_name>]

This command is to display the FEC status of the selected interfaces. If **interface_name** is not specicied, this command shows the FEC status of all interfaces.
Command: show interfaces fec status [<interface_name>]

This command is to display the link-training status of the selected interfaces. If **interface_name** is not specicied, this command shows the link-training status of all interfaces.
Command: show interfaces link-training status [<interface_name>]

The show interfaces flap command provides detailed insights into interface events, including the timestamp of the last link down event and the total flap count (number of times the link has gone up and down). This helps in diagnosing stability and connectivity issues.
Command: show interfaces flap

The show interface errors command provides detailed statistics and error counters for MAC-level operations on an interface. It displays the status of various operational parameters, error counts, and timestamps for when these errors occurred.
Command: show interfaces errors [<interface_name>]

This command is used to display the configured MPLS state for the list of configured interfaces.
Command: show interfaces mpls [<interface_name>]

This command displays the configured loopback action
Command: show ip interfaces loopback-action

This command displays the key fields of the interfaces such as Operational Status, Administrative Status, Alias and TPID.
Command: show interfaces tpid [<interface_name>]

Refer sub-section [Interface-Naming-Mode](#Interface-Naming-Mode)
Command: show interfaces neighbor expected [<interface_name>] -n [<namespace>]

This command displays information regarding port-channel interfaces
Command: show interfaces portchannel

This command displays some more fields such as Lanes, Speed, MTU, Type, Asymmetric PFC status and also the operational and administrative status of the interfaces
Command: show interfaces status [<interface_name>]

This command displays switchport modes status of the interfaces
Command: show interfaces switchport status

This command displays switchport modes configuration of the interfaces
Command: show interfaces switchport config

This command is used to enable or disable low-power mode for an SFP transceiver
Command: config interface transceiver lpmode <interface_name> (enable | disable)

This command is used to set the coherent module laser frequency
Command: config interface transceiver frequency <interface_name> <frequency_value>

This command is used to set the coherent module tx power
Command: config interface transceiver tx_power <interface_name> <tx_power_value>

This command is used to reset an SFP transceiver
Command: config interface transceiver reset <interface_name>

This command is used to configure the Digital Optical Monitoring (DOM) for an interface.
Command: config interface transceiver dom <interface_name> (enable | disable)

This command is used to set active breakout mode available for user-specified interface based on the platform-specific port configuration file(i.e. platform.json) and the current mode set for the interface.

This command displays the brief summary of all LLDP neighbors.
Command: show lldp table

This command displays more details about all LLDP neighbors or only the neighbors connected to a specific interface.
Command: show lldp neighbors <interface_name>

This command is used to load the configuration from a JSON file like the file which SONiC saves its configuration to, `/etc/sonic/config_db.json` This command loads the configuration from the input file (if user specifies this optional filename, it will use that input file. Otherwise, it will use the default `/etc/sonic/config_db.json` file as the input file) into CONFIG_DB. The configuration present in the input file is applied on top of the already running configuration. This command does not flush the config DB before loading the new configuration (i.e., If the configuration present in the input file is same as the current running configuration, nothing happens) If the config present in the input file is not present in running configuration, it will be added. If the config present in the input file differs (when key matches) from that of the running configuration, it will be modified as per the new values for those keys.
Command: config load [-y|--yes] [<filename>]

This command is used to load the configuration from /etc/sonic/minigraph.xml. When users do not want to use configuration from config_db.json, they can copy the minigraph.xml configuration file to the device and load it using this command. This command restarts various services running in the device and it takes some time to complete the command.
Command: config load_minigraph [-y|--yes] [-n|--no-service-restart] [-t|--traffic-shift-away]

This command is used to clear current configuration and import new configurationn from the input file or from /etc/sonic/config_db.json. This command shall stop all services before clearing the configuration and it then restarts those services.
Command: config reload [-y|--yes] [-l|--load-sysinfo] [<filename>] [-n|--no-service-restart] [-f|--force]

This command is used to reconfigure hostname and mgmt interface based on device description file. This command either uses the optional file specified as arguement or looks for the file "/etc/sonic/device_desc.xml". If the file does not exist or if the file does not have valid fields for "hostname" and "ManagementAddress" (or "ManagementAddressV6"), it fails.
Command: config load_mgmt_config [-y|--yes] [<filename>]

This command is to save the config DB configuration into the user-specified filename or into the default /etc/sonic/config_db.json. This saves the configuration into the disk which is available even after reboots. Saved file can be transferred to remote machines for debugging. If users wants to load the configuration from this new file at any point of time, they can use "config load" command and provide this newly generated file as input. If users wants this newly generated file to be used during reboot, they need to copy this file to /etc/sonic/config_db.json.
Command: config save [-y|--yes] [<filename>]

This command is used to add or delete loopback interfaces. It is recommended to use loopback names in the format "Loopbackxxx", where "xxx" is number of 1 to 3 digits. Ex: "Loopback11".
Command: config loopback (add | del) <loopback_name>

This command displays all vrfs configured on the system along with interface binding to the vrf. If vrf-name is also provided as part of the command, if the vrf is created it will display all interfaces binding to the vrf, if vrf is not created nothing will be displayed.
Command: show vrf [<vrf_name>]

This command creates vrf in SONiC system with provided vrf-name.
Command: config vrf add <vrf-name>

This command deletes vrf with name vrf-name.
Command: config vrf del <vrf-name>

This command displays whether the management VRF is enabled or disabled. It also displays the details about the the links (eth0, mgmt, lo-m) that are related to management VRF.
Command: show mgmt-vrf

This command displays the routes that are present in the routing table 5000 that is meant for management VRF.
Command: show mgmt-vrf routes

This command displays the IP address(es) configured for the management interface "eth0" and the management network default gateway.
Command: show management_interface address

This command displays the configured SNMP agent IP addresses.
Command: show snmpagentaddress

This command displays the configured SNMP Trap server IP addresses.
Command: show snmptrap

This command enables the management VRF in the system. This command restarts the "interfaces-config" service which in turn regenerates the /etc/network/interfaces file and restarts the "networking" service. This creates a new interface and l3mdev CGROUP with the name as "mgmt" and enslaves the management interface "eth0" into this master interface "mgmt". Note that the VRFName "mgmt" (or "management") is reserved for management VRF. i.e. Data VRFs should not use these reserved VRF names.
Command: config vrf add mgmt

This command disables the management VRF in the system. This command restarts the "interfaces-config" service which in turn regenerates the /etc/network/interfaces file and restarts the "networking" service. This deletes the interface "mgmt" and deletes the l3mdev CGROUP named "mgmt" and puts back the management interface "eth0" into the default VRF. Note that the VRFName "mgmt" (or "management") is reserved for management VRF. i.e. Data VRFs should not use these reserved VRF names.
Command: config vrf del mgmt

This command adds the SNMP agent IP address on which the SNMP agent is expected to listen. When SNMP agent is expected to work as part of management VRF, users should specify the optional vrf_name parameter as "mgmt". This configuration goes into snmpd.conf that is used by SNMP agent. SNMP service is restarted to make this configuration effective in SNMP agent.
Command: config snmpagentaddress add [-p <port_num>] [-v <vrf_name>] agentip

This command deletes the SNMP agent IP address on which the SNMP agent is expected to listen. When users had added the agent IP as part of "mgmt" VRF, users should specify the optional vrf_name parameter as "mgmt" while deleting as well. This configuration is removed from snmpd.conf that is used by SNMP agent. SNMP service is restarted to make this configuration effective in SNMP agent.
Command: config snmpagentaddress del [-p <port_num>] [-v <vrf_name>] agentip

This command modifies the SNMP trap server IP address to which the SNMP agent is expected to send the traps. Users can configure one server IP addrss for each SNMP version to send the traps. When SNMP agent is expected to send traps as part of management VRF, users should specify the optional vrf_name parameter as "mgmt". This configuration goes into snmpd.conf that is used by SNMP agent. SNMP service is restarted to make this configuration effective in SNMP agent.
Command: config snmptrap modify <snmp_version> [-p <port_num>] [-v <vrf_name>] [-c <community>] trapserverip

This command deletes the SNMP Trap server IP address to which SNMP agent is expected to send TRAPs. When users had added the trap server IP as part of "mgmt" VRF, users should specify the optional vrf_name parameter as "mgmt" while deleting as well. This configuration is removed from snmpd.conf that is used by SNMP agent. SNMP service is restarted to make this configuration effective in SNMP agent.
Command: config snmptrap del [-p <port_num>] [-v <vrf_name>] [-c <community>] trapserverip

This command displays all the status of either all the ports which are connected to muxcable or any individual port selected by the user. The resultant table or json output will show the current status of muxcable on the port (auto/active) and also the health of the muxcable.
Command: show muxcable status [OPTIONS] [PORT]

This command displays all the configurations of either all the ports which are connected to muxcable or any individual port selected by the user. The resultant table or json output will show the current configurations of muxcable on the port(active/standby) and also the ipv4 and ipv6 address of the port as well as peer TOR ip address with the hostname.
Command: show muxcable config [OPTIONS] [PORT]

This command displays the ber(Bit error rate) of the port user provides on the target user provides. The target provided as an integer corresponds to actual target as. 0 -> local 1 -> tor 1 2 -> tor 2 3 -> nic
Command: Usage: show muxcable ber-info [OPTIONS] PORT TARGET

This command displays the eye info in mv(milli volts) of the port user provides on the target user provides. The target provided as an integer corresponds to actual target as. 0 -> local 1 -> tor 1 2 -> tor 2 3 -> nic
Command: Usage: show muxcable eye-info [OPTIONS] PORT TARGET

This command displays the hardware health of  the Y-cable which are connected to muxcable. The resultant table or json output will show the current hadrware health of the cable as Ok, Not Ok, Unknown.
Command: show muxcable health [OPTIONS] [PORT]

This command displays the queue info of  the Y-cable which are connected to muxcable. The resultant table or json output will show the queue info in terms transactions for the UART stats in particular currently relevant to the MCU of the cable.
Command: show muxcable queueinfo [OPTIONS] [PORT]

This command displays the operationtime of  the Y-cable which are connected to muxcable. The resultant table or json output will show the current operation time of the cable as `hh:mm:ss` format. Operation time means the time since the last time the reseated/reset of the cable is done, and the time would be in the format specified
Command: show muxcable operationtime [OPTIONS] [PORT]

This command displays the resetcause of  the Y-cable which are connected to muxcable. The resultant table or json output will show the most recent reset cause of the cable as string format.
Command: show muxcable resetcause [OPTIONS] [PORT]

This command is used for setting the configuration of a muxcable Port/all ports to be active or auto. The user has to enter a port number or else all to make the muxcable config operation on all the ports. Depending on the status of the muxcable port state the resultant output could be OK or INPROGRESS . OK would imply no change on the state, INPROGRESS would mean the toggle is happening in the background.
Command: config muxcable mode [OPTIONS] <operation_status> <port_name>

This command is used for setting the configuration and enable/diable of prbs on a port user provides. While enabling in addition to port the user also needs to provides the target, prbs mode and lane map on which the user intends to run prbs on. The target reflects where the enable/dsiable will happen.
Command: config muxcable prbs enable [OPTIONS] PORT TARGET MODE_VALUE LANE_MAP
  config muxcable prbs disable [OPTIONS] PORT TARGET

This command is used for setting the configuration and enable/disable of loopback on a port user provides. While enabling in addition to port the user also needs to provides the target and lane map on which the user intends to run loopback on. The target reflects where the enable/dsiable will happen.
Command: config muxcable loopback enable [OPTIONS] PORT TARGET LANE_MAP
  config muxcable loopback disable [OPTIONS] PORT TARGET

This command displays all the mirror sessions that are configured.
Command: show mirror_session

This command is used to add or remove mirroring sessions. Mirror session is identified by "session_name". This command supports configuring both SPAN/ERSPAN sessions. In SPAN user can configure mirroring of list of source ports/LAG to destination port in ingress/egress/both directions. In ERSPAN user can configure mirroring of list of source ports/LAG to a destination IP. Both SPAN/ERSPAN support ACL based mirroring and can be used in ACL configurations.
Command: config mirror_session erspan add <session_name> <src_ip> <dst_ip> <dscp> <ttl> [gre_type] [queue] [policer <policer_name>] [source-port-list] [direction]

Command: config mmu [OPTIONS]

This command displays the NAT configuration.
Command: show nat config [static | pool | bindings | globalvalues | zones]

This command displays the NAT translation statistics for each entry.
Command: show nat statistics

This command displays the NAT translation entries.
Command: show nat translations [count]

This command is used to add a static NAT or NAPT entry. When configuring the Static NAT entry, user has to specify the following fields with 'basic' keyword.
Command: config nat add static {{basic (global-ip) (local-ip)} | {{tcp | udp} (global-ip) (global-port) (local-ip) (local-port)}} [-nat_type {snat | dnat}] [-twice_nat_id (value)]

This command is used to create a NAT pool used for dynamic Source NAT or NAPT translations. Pool can be configured in one of the following combinations.
Command: config nat add pool (pool-name) (global-ip-range) (global-port-range)

This command is used to create a NAT binding between a pool and an ACL. The following fields are needed for configuring the binding.
Command: config nat add binding (binding-name) [(pool-name)] [(acl-name)] [-nat_type {snat | dnat}] [-twice_nat_id (value)]

This command is used to configure NAT zone on an L3 interface. Default value of NAT zone on an L3 interface is 0. Valid range of zone values is 0-3.
Command: config nat add interface (interface-name) -nat_zone (value)

This command is used to set the NAT timeout values. Different timeout values can be configured for the NAT entry timeout, NAPT TCP entry timeout, NAPT UDP entry timeout. Range for Global NAT entry timeout is 300 sec to 432000 sec, default value is 600 sec. Range for TCP NAT/NAPT entry timeout is 300 sec to 432000 sec, default value is 86400 sec. Range for UDP NAT/NAPT entry timeout is 120 sec to 600 sec, default value is 300 sec.
Command: config nat set {tcp-timeout (value) | timeout (value) | udp-timeout (value)}

This command is used to enable or disable the NAT feature.
Command: config nat feature {enable | disable}

This command is used to clear the dynamic NAT and NAPT translation entries.
Command: sonic-clear nat translations

This command is used to clear the statistics of all the NAT and NAPT entries.
Command: sonic-clear nat statistics

This command is used to reload the QoS configuration. QoS configuration has got two sets of configurations. 1) Generic QOS Configuration - This gives complete list of all possible QOS configuration. This command displays the global sFlow configuration that includes the admin state, collectors, the Agent ID and counter polling interval.

Command: config sflow collector add <collector-name> <ipv4-address | ipv6-address> [port <number>]

This command is used to delete a sFlow collector with the given name.
Command: config sflow collector del <collector-name>

This command is used to add/delete the sFlow agent-id. This setting is global (applicable to both collectors) and optional. Only a single agent-id is allowed. If agent-id is not specified (with this CLI), an appropriate IP that belongs to the switch is used as the agent-id based on some simple heuristics.
Command: config sflow agent-id <add|del> <interface-name>

Globally, sFlow is disabled by default. When sFlow is enabled globally, the sflow deamon is started and sampling will start on all interfaces which have sFlow enabled at the interface level (see “config sflow interface…”). When sflow is disabled globally, sampling is stopped on all relevant interfaces and sflow daemon is stopped.
Command: config sflow <enable|disable>

This command takes global sflow sample direction. If not configured, default is "rx" for backward compatibility. Based on the direction, the sFlow is enabled at all the interface level at rx or tx or both.
Command: config sflow sample-direction <rx|tx|both>

Enable/disable sflow at an interface level. By default, sflow is enabled on all interfaces at the interface level. Use this command to explicitly disable sFlow for a specific interface. An interface is sampled if sflow is enabled globally as well as at the interface level. Note that this configuration deals only with sFlow flow samples and not counter samples.
Command: config sflow interface <enable|disable> <interface-name|all>

Set sample direction to determine ingress sampling or egress sampling or both. If not configured, default is "rx".
Command: config sflow sample-direction <interface-name|all> <rx|tx|both>

Configure the sample-rate for a specific interface.
Command: config sflow interface sample-rate <interface-name> <value>

This command is used to set the counter polling interval. Default is 20 seconds.
Command: config sflow polling-interval <value>

This command displays all the configuration related to warm_restart.
Command: show warm_restart config

This command displays the warm_restart state.
Command: show warm_restart state

This command is used to set the bgp_timer value for warm_restart of BGP service. bgp_timer holds the time interval utilized by fpmsyncd during warm-restart episodes. During this interval fpmsyncd will recover all the routing state previously pushed to AppDB, as well as all the new state coming from zebra/bgpd. Upon expiration of this timer, fpmsyncd will execute the reconciliation logic to eliminate all the stale entries from AppDB. This timer should match the BGP-GR restart-timer configured within the elected routing-stack. Supported range: 1-3600.
Command: config warm_restart [-s|--redis-unix-socket-path <socket_path>] bgp_timer <seconds>

This command is used to enable or disable the warm_restart for a particular service that supports warm reboot. Following four services support warm reboot. When user restarts the particular service using "systemctl restart <service_name>", this configured value will be checked for whether it is enabled or disabled. If this configuration is enabled for that service, it will perform warm reboot for that service. Otherwise, it will do cold restart of the service.
Command: config warm_restart [-s|--redis-unix-socket-path <socket_path>] enable [<module_name>]

This command is used to set the neighsyncd_timer value for warm_restart of "swss" service. neighsyncd_timer is the timer used for "swss" (neighsyncd) service during the warm restart. Timer is started after the neighborTable is restored to internal data structures. neighborsyncd then starts to read all Linux kernel entries and mark the entries in the data structures accordingly. Once the timer is expired, reconciliation is done and the delta is pushed to appDB Valid value is 1-9999. 0 is invalid.
Command: config warm_restart [-s|--redis-unix-socket-path <socket_path>] neighsyncd_timer <seconds>

This command is used to set the bgp_timer value for warm_restart of "bgp" service. bgp_timer is the timer used for "bgp" service during the warm restart. Timer is started after the BGP table is restored to internal data structures. BGP services then start to read all Linux kernel entries and mark the entries in the data structures accordingly. Once the timer is expired, reconciliation is done and the delta is pushed to appDB Valid value is 1-9999. 0 is invalid.
Command: config warm_restart [-s|--redis-unix-socket-path <socket_path>] bgp_timer <seconds>

This command is used to set the teamsyncd_timer value for warm_restart of teamd service. teamsyncd_timer holds the time interval utilized by teamsyncd during warm-restart episodes. The timer is started when teamsyncd starts. During the timer interval, teamsyncd will preserve all LAG interface changes, but it will not apply them. The changes will only be applied when the timer expires. When the changes are applied, the stale LAG entries will be removed, the new LAG entries will be created. Supported range: 1-9999. 0 is invalid
Command: config warm_restart teamsyncd_timer <seconds>

This command lists all available SONiC packages, their desription, installed version and installation status. SONiC package status can be *Installed*, *Not installed* or *Built-In*. "Built-In" status means that a feature is built-in to SONiC image and can't be upgraded or uninstalled.
Command: sonic-package-manager list

This command will add a new repository as source for SONiC packages to the database. *NOTE*: requires elevated (root) privileges to run
Command: Usage: sonic-package-manager repository add [OPTIONS] NAME REPOSITORY

    Add a new repository to database.

    NOTE: This command requires elevated (root) privileges to run.

  Options:
    --default-reference TEXT  Default installation reference. Can be a tag or
                              sha256 digest in repository.
    --description TEXT        Optional package entry description.
    --help                    Show this message and exit.

This command will remove a repository as source for SONiC packages from the database . The package has to be *Not Installed* in order to be removed from package database. *NOTE*: requires elevated (root) privileges to run
Command: Usage: sonic-package-manager repository remove [OPTIONS] NAME

    Remove repository from database.

    NOTE: This command requires elevated (root) privileges to run.

  Options:
    --help  Show this message and exit.

This command pulls and installs a package on SONiC host. *NOTE*: this command requires elevated (root) privileges to run
Command: Usage: sonic-package-manager install [OPTIONS] [PACKAGE_EXPR]

    Install/Upgrade package using [PACKAGE_EXPR] in format
    "<name>[=<version>|@<reference>]".

      The repository to pull the package from is resolved by lookup in
      package database,    thus the package has to be added via "sonic-
      package-manager repository add" command.

      In case when [PACKAGE_EXPR] is a package name "<name>" this command
      will install or upgrade    to a version referenced by "default-
      reference" in package database.

    NOTE: This command requires elevated (root) privileges to run.

  Options:
    --enable                  Set the default state of the feature to enabled
                              and enable feature right after installation. NOTE:
                              user needs to execute "config save -y" to make
                              this setting persistent.
    --set-owner [local|kube]  Default owner configuration setting for a feature.
    --from-repository TEXT    Fetch package directly from image registry
                              repository. NOTE: This argument is mutually
                              exclusive with arguments: [package_expr,
                              from_tarball].
    --from-tarball FILE       Fetch package from saved image tarball. NOTE: This
                              argument is mutually exclusive with arguments:
                              [package_expr, from_repository].
    -f, --force               Force operation by ignoring package dependency
                              tree and package manifest validation failures.
    -y, --yes                 Automatically answer yes on prompts.
    -v, --verbosity LVL       Either CRITICAL, ERROR, WARNING, INFO or DEBUG.
                              Default is INFO.
    --skip-host-plugins       Do not install host OS plugins provided by the
                              package (CLI, etc). NOTE: In case when package
                              host OS plugins are set as mandatory in package
                              manifest this option will fail the installation.
    --allow-downgrade         Allow package downgrade. By default an attempt to
                              downgrade the package will result in a failure
                              since downgrade might not be supported by the
                              package, thus requires explicit request from the
                              user.
    --help                    Show this message and exit..

This command uninstalls package from SONiC host. User needs to stop the feature prior to uninstalling it. *NOTE*: this command requires elevated (root) privileges to run.

  Options:

    -y, --yes            Automatically answer yes on prompts.
    -v, --verbosity LVL  Either CRITICAL, ERROR, WARNING, INFO or DEBUG. Default
